APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes. The paper addresses an NLP/ML problem (deceptive review detection) using core ML/NLP methods: a CNN-based text encoder jointly trained with a TransE-style graph embedding to encode reviewer/product/review interactions (Sections 4.1–4.3). It evaluates on a real-world benchmark (Yelp) with standard metrics (P/R/F1/Accuracy) and compares to linguistic and behavioral baselines (Sections 5.1, Tables 4–5), and grounds itself in relevant prior work on linguistic and behavioral features, graph embeddings, and representation learning. Overall the topic, audience, problem formulation, methods, and evaluation are directly appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has an overall logical structure (intro → method → experiments) but is poorly presented: many paragraphs and citations are duplicated, several table/figure references are misspelled (e.g., “Tabel”), and parts of the text are fragmented or awkward (e.g., “they couldn’t form themselves or make no sense”, “in after weeks”), which disrupts flow. Main contributions are mentioned but not cleanly or prominently stated early; they are buried in repetitious background material. Notation for the TransE-style model is mostly given (β, α, τ and loss terms), but presentation is inconsistent (norm constraints and sets are scattered) and some equations/constraints are awkwardly formatted. Figures/tables are frequently cited to support claims but are not shown here and their labels/text contain typos, reducing clarity; overall the writing has many grammatical errors and redundancies that need substantial editing.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a clearly important and operationally urgent problem—detecting review spam at cold-start—stated explicitly as “an urgent and significant task” and proposes a concrete solution that augments sparse behavioral signals by jointly embedding text and global review-graph behavior (TransE-style) with CNN text encodings (Equations (1)-(5) and Section 4.1–4.3). Reported empirical gains (≈9.0% F1 and ≈3.8% accuracy improvements on Yelp; Sec. 5.4) suggest the approach could change how practitioners combine textual and behavioral signals for early detection. Because the method is scalable/unsupervised and claims domain adaptability, it is likely to be adopted and to spawn follow-on work applying joint embeddings to other cold-start or behavior-augmented classification problems.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states a clear main claim — jointly embedding textual and behavioral information via a TransE-like model plus a CNN improves cold-start spam detection — and presents empirical evidence (e.g. §5.4: “around 9.0% improvement in F1 and around 3.8% improvement in A in the hotel domain” vs a CNN-only baseline). The mathematical formulation (Eqns. 1–5) is reasonable and internally consistent as a margin-ranking objective with norm constraints, but important implementation details (how norms are enforced, choice of squared L2 vs. TransE’s L1/L2, negative-sampling strategy S′, and the rating-corruption mapping in Γ′) are underspecified. The experimental methodology compares sensible baselines (bigrams, behavioral features, CNN-only) on a temporally split Yelp corpus, but lacks reported statistical significance, hyperparameter/ablation details (e.g. θ), and full description of training/validation controls, leaving some claims (e.g. robustness/domain-adaptability and unsupervised applicability) not fully supported.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments use a real Yelp split and compare baselines (LF bigrams, behavioral features, BF abundant) and variants (independent CNN vs joint embeddings), reporting concrete gains (e.g. ~9% F1 and ~3.8% accuracy improvements in Table 5) and domain differences (hotel vs restaurant). Ablation-style comparisons are present (behavioral sparse vs abundant, independent CNN, variants like BF EditSim/BF W2Vsim, and some embedding variants in Table 4), and related work is extensively cited. However, the paper lacks sensitivity analyses (no hyperparameter sweeps or θ/embedding-dimension/margin/CNN-architecture ablation), limited error analysis or explicit failure-case discussion, and omits many reproduction details (CNN architecture, training hyperparameters, optimization and statistical significance), so the experimental evaluation is adequate but missing important analyses.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper addresses a specific, under-explored setting (immediate cold-start detection of a new reviewer) and proposes a concrete, different approach: jointly learning CNN-based review text embeddings with global behavioral information using a TransE-like triplet embedding (product, reviewer, review) and additional rating-embedding constraints (Equations (1)–(5)). This combination (encoding reviewers as translation vectors to augment sparse cold-start signals and jointly training rating embeddings) is a non-trivial integration beyond standard linguistic or behavioral feature methods, and the authors explicitly contrast it with prior behavioral-statistics approaches and existing representation work (e.g., Wang et al. (2016)). While it builds on known components (CNN, TransE), the problem focus and joint modeling provide notable novelty rather than a purely incremental contribution.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against reasonable baselines: SVMs with linguistic bigrams and three behavioral features (RL, RD, MCS) (Table 1), intuitive approximations (BF EditSim+LF, BF W2Vsim+W2V, Table 4), and an independent supervised CNN with the same structure as the CNN part (Section 5.4 / Table 5), all evaluated on the same Yelp split and metrics (P/R/F1/A) used for the cold-start task. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the paper explicitly discusses prior findings (e.g., Mukherjee et al. 2013 showing bigrams are strong), but it omits direct experimental comparisons to some recent/closely related graph/embedding or behavioral-method baselines (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 and other contemporary behavioral models like Rayana & Akoglu 2015) despite citing them, which are relevant omissions.
